claims.claim: We evaluate whether a local generative language model can serve as an effective reader on a validated natural context-overflow benchmark, where an answer token must be extracted from long-form documents containing natural distractor text.

evidence_refs.quote: # Context Overflow Real LLM Reader Validation

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: A Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct model achieves 999/1000 exact answer-token accuracy (0.999) on the full 1,000-example dataset, with complete output parseability, outperforming lexical retrieval (0.903), TF-IDF retrieval (0.785), context truncation at 4,096 tokens (0.85), and a boundary-probe baseline at 4,096 tokens (0.95).

claims.claim: A smaller Qwen2.5-0.5B-Instruct model achieves only 88/100 accuracy on a 100-example pilot subset, tying TF-IDF retrieval and exhibiting unparseable-output failures on technical distractor text.

claims.claim: The single error produced by the 3B model is a generative copy error in which a nearby numeric distractor was substituted for the correct answer token.
